subject: Mathematics
solution:
We know that the conditional statement a⇒b is False only when a is False and b is True.

Hence, p⇒(q∨r): False when p:True;  q∨r : False

Again, we know that a∨b is False when both a & b are false.

Hence, p:True; q:False; r: False.

solution: Contrapositive of "If $A$ then $B^{\prime \prime}$ is "If $\sim \mathrm{B}$ then $\sim A^{\prime \prime}$. Hence contrapositive of "If two numbers are not equal, then their squares are not equal" is "If squares of two numbers are equal, then the two numbers are equal".

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Logical Statement p→q is false if and only if p is true and q is false.

So, when p is false, q is true, p∨q→p∨~q will be false.

Clearly p∨q→p∨~q is not a tautology.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given condition is, p∧q→r∨q∧p∧r→q).....(1)

We know conditional disjunction as,

p→q≡~p∨q

Hence, p∧r→q≡~p∧r∨q

Using De Morgan's law

p∧r→q≡~p∧r∨q≡~p∨~r∨q

Equation 1 can be written as,

⇒p∧q→r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∧q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨~q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒(~p∨~q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨t∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨~r∨q

For above statement to be a tautology,

r can be ~p or q

When r=~p or r=q⇒~p∨~r∨q=t

∴ Two value of r are possible

subject: Mathematics
options: The match will not be played and weather is not good and ground is wet.
If the match will not be played, then either weather is not good or ground is wet.
The match will be played and weather is not good or ground is wet.
The match will not be played or weather is good and ground is not wet.
solution:
Let p: weather is good
and q: ground is not wet

Given statement "The match will be played only if the weather is good and ground is not wet" can be written as p∧q

Now, negation of  p∧q is ~p∧q≡~p∨~q

which is equivalent to "The match will be played and weather is not good or ground is wet".

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Using logical equivalence condition p→q≡~p∨q, given statement can be expressed as, 

p∧~q→p→~q

≡ ~p∧~q∨~p∨~q.......(1)

We know De Morgan's law of logical equivalence is,

~p∧q≡~p∨~q

Applying De Morgan's law in equation(1) we get,

≡ ~p∨q∨~p∨~q   ∵~~p≡p

≡ ~p∨~p∨q∨~q

≡~p∨t≡t (tautology)

subject: Mathematics
options: if I will go to the doctor, then I am not feeling well.
if I am feeling well, then I will not go to the doctor.
if I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
if I will go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
solution:
Statement: If I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor.

Let p: I am not feeling well and q: I will go to the doctor.

The given statement is p→q.

We know that the contrapositive of p→q is ~q→~p.

Contrapositive: If I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.

(p⇒q)∧(q⇒~p)

=(~p∨q)∧(~q∨~p)  {p⇒q=~p∨q}

=(~p∨q)∧(~p∨~q)  {commutative property}

=~p∨(q∧~q)   {distributive property}

=~p

then F⇒F≡F

i.e. S1 is not a tautology

S2:  If p is T, q is F, r is F , then

p∨q⇒r≡F

then p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r≡F

i.e. S2 is not a tautology

subject: Mathematics
options: If a function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is continuous at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>, then it is not differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>.

If a function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is not continuous at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>, then it is not differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>.

If a function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is not continuous at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>. then it is differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>.

If a function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is continuous at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>, then it is differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>.
solution:
By using definition, we have;

Contrapositive of p⇒q is ~q⇒~p.

Which means contrapositive of given statement is;

"If a function f is not continuous at a, then it is not differentiable at a."

solution:
Given,

((A∧(B∨C))⇒(A∨B))⇒A

We know that, X⇒Y≡~X∨Y,

Now using the above formula we get,

≡~(A∧(B∨C))∨(A∨B)⇒A

≡~(~(A∧(B∨C))∨(A∨B))∨A

≡(A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B)∨A

Now to solve (A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B) let us assume a universal set 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8, set A=1,2,3, set B=3,4,5 and set C=5,6,7

So, A∧B∨C will be 3 and ~(A∨B) will be 6,7,8, 

So, (A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B)=∅

≡F∨A

≡A

∴ Negation of statement=~A
